perturbation.original_text: As used herein, “ Confidential Information ” shall mean any and all technical and non-technical information previously, presently, or subsequently disclosed or provided by Disclosing Party and/or one or more of its Affiliates to Receiving Party and/or one or more of its Affiliates in written, oral or electronic form.
perturbation.changed_text: As used herein, “ Confidential Information ” shall mean any and all technical and non-technical information previously, presently, or subsequently disclosed or provided by Disclosing Party and/or one or more of its Affiliates to Receiving Party and/or one or more of its Affiliates in written form only.
perturbation.explanation: By restricting confidential information to only written forms, it conflicts with the broad definition of trade secrets that includes electronic and oral disclosures, potentially leaving those forms of communication unprotected.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) protects trade secrets, which can be in any form.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA defines trade secrets broadly, encompassing information in any form. Limiting the definition of 'Confidential Information' to 'written form only' directly contradicts this inclusive definition, reducing the scope of protection offered by the NDA compared to the legal definition.

Section Status USC Title USC Section Authorizes 1 18 1 nt 2(f) 18 1833 nt 2(g) 18 1833 nt 2(e) 18 1833 nt 2(c) 18 1833 2(b) 18 1839 2(a) 18 1836 3(b) 18 1961 4 34 41310 6 28 620 nt 7(b) 18 1838 7(a) 18 1833 2(d)(1) 18 1836 3(a)(1) 18 1832 3(a)(2) 18 1835 TOPN: Table of Popular Names Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the definition of 'Confidential Information' to include only written forms, while the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), as suggested by the title of 'Scraped Law Snippet 1', broadly encompasses information in any form. The 'Perturbation Explanation' and 'Law Explanation' also support this. By limiting the definition, the modified contract creates a legal contradiction because it reduces the scope of protection for trade secrets compared to the inclusive legal definition as indicated by the DTSA.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding clause (f) , Disclosing Party may seek injunctive relief in any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding clause (f) , Disclosing Party may only seek monetary damages. Injunctive relief is not an available remedy.
perturbation.explanation: This change eliminates the possibility of seeking injunctive relief for breaches, which is a standard remedy for violations of NDAs and trade secret misappropriation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California law allows for injunctive relief in cases of trade secret misappropriation.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 3426.2 explicitly provides for injunctive relief to prevent or restrain actual or threatened misappropriation of trade secrets. The modified text, by denying access to injunctive relief, directly contradicts this statutory provision, limiting the remedies available to the Disclosing Party compared to what California law allows.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.2 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.2. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly prohibits seeking injunctive relief, which is a direct contradiction of California Civil Code Section 3426.2(a). The law states that "Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined," meaning that injunctive relief is an available remedy. The modification removes this remedy, directly conflicting with the statute.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall not transfer or assign any rights or obligations under this Agreement without the prior written consent of Disclosing Party, which consent may be given or withheld in Disclosing Party’s sole and absolute discretion.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall have the right to assign this agreement to any subsidiary without the consent of the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: This allows assignment to a subsidiary without consent, undermining the control the disclosing party has over who receives confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract law principles generally require mutual consent for assignment unless the contract explicitly states otherwise.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1457 implies that the burden of an obligation may be transferred with the consent of the party entitled to its benefit. The modified text allows assignment to a subsidiary without the Disclosing Party's consent, violating this principle of contract law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1457 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 1. OBLIGATIONS IN GENERAL [1427 - 1543] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. TRANSFER OF OBLIGATIONS [1457 - 1471] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1457. The burden of an obligation may be transferred with the consent of the party entitled to its benefit, but not otherwise, except as provided by Section 1466. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates written consent from the Disclosing Party before the Receiving Party can transfer any rights or obligations. The changed text allows the Receiving Party to assign the agreement to a subsidiary without the Disclosing Party's consent. This directly contradicts California Civil Code Section 1457, which states, "The burden of an obligation may be transferred with the consent of the party entitled to its benefit, but not otherwise."

perturbation.original_text: The term of this Agreement commences on the Effective Date and ends on the date one (1) year thereafter. Receiving Party’s obligations to protect Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement shall survive termination of this Agreement and will be binding upon Receiving Party, its heirs, successors, and assigns for a period of five (5) years from expiration or termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The term of this Agreement commences on the Effective Date and ends on the date one (1) year thereafter. Receiving Party’s obligations to protect Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement shall not survive termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the survival clause eliminates any obligation to protect confidential information after the agreement terminates, effectively negating the purpose of the NDA after one year.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Trade secret law protects confidential information indefinitely as long as it remains a secret and provides a competitive advantage.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a statute, the Restatement of Torts Section 757 is highly influential in trade secret law and establishes that the protection of trade secrets continues as long as the information remains confidential and provides a competitive advantage. By removing the survival clause, the agreement fails to provide ongoing protection, potentially conflicting with the principles of trade secret law and rendering the confidential information vulnerable after the agreement's initial term.

perturbation.justification: The original contract ensures the protection of confidential information for five years post-termination, while the changed version eliminates this protection entirely. The Law Explanation states that the removal of the survival clause fails to provide ongoing protection, potentially conflicting with the principles of trade secret law and rendering the confidential information vulnerable after the agreement's initial term. This change directly contradicts the principle of ongoing protection for trade secrets, as explained, by negating any obligation to safeguard confidential information after the initial term.
